RECONSTRUCTED FRAMEWORK (flagged, not a verified translation):

Cobo treats trigo (wheat) in the sections of Historia del Nuevo Mundo devoted to Old World plants introduced to the Americas, distinguishing it from native grains such as maize (maíz) and quinoa. He notes that wheat was among the earliest European cereals to be cultivated successfully in New Spain and Peru, where the climate of highland valleys proved favourable. Cobo records that Spanish settlers and religious houses depended on wheat for bread [pan de Castilla], which was considered nutritionally superior to maize bread by European physicians of the period. Medicinally, Cobo and his contemporaries drew on Galenic tradition: wheat flour was held to be temperate and nourishing, bread prepared from well-sifted flour [harina cernida] was prescribed for convalescents and for those suffering from weaknesses of the stomach [flaqueza de estómago]; wheat bran [salvado] boiled in water or vinegar was applied as a poultice to reduce external swellings and inflammations [hinchazones]; and wheat starch [almidón de trigo] was used in emollient preparations for irritated skin. The grain was not assigned Quechua, Aymara, or Nahuatl names in Cobo's account because it was a post-contact introduction with no indigenous tradition of cultivation.

results.title_inferred: Vilca: Andean Hallucinogenic Snuff Plant (Anadenanthera colubrina)
results.original_text: La vilca es árbol conocido en todo el Perú, aunque más usado entre las naciones del Collao y de los valles calientes. Llámanla los Ingas vilca, y en algunas provincias huilca; los Aymaras la nombran también con este mesmo nombre. Es árbol de mediana grandeza, de corteza áspera y madera recia; las hojas son menudas y la flor amarillenta. Críase en los valles templados y en las quebradas abrigadas de los Andes. El fruto son unas vainas largas y retorcidas, dentro de las cuales están unas pepitas o semillas pardas y algo aplanadas. De estas semillas usan los indios para hacer un polvo con que se embriagan aspirándolo por las narices, lo cual llaman tomar vilca. Muélenlas después de tostadas y mezclan el polvo con tabaco u otras yerbas, y así preparado lo aspiran con unos cañutos de hueso o de caña puestos en las narices. Causa este polvo un sueño profundo y visiones, durante las cuales los hechiceros y adivinos dicen comunicarse con sus dioses y conocer las cosas ocultas y futuras. Úsanla también para purgar, porque tomada en bebida tiene virtud de purgar el vientre poderosamente. Los médicos de aquella tierra la aplican asimismo, cocida en agua, para lavativas en casos de obstrucción y males del vientre. La corteza del árbol, machacada y puesta en los ríos o pozas, aturde y embriaga a los peces, que suben atontados a la superficie, y así los pescan fácilmente los indios.
results.translation: The vilca is a tree known throughout all of Peru, though more commonly used among the peoples of the Collao [the Titicaca basin region] and the warm valleys. The Incas call it vilca, and in some provinces huilca; the Aymara peoples likewise refer to it by this same name. It is a tree of middling size, with rough bark and hard wood; the leaves are small and the flower yellowish. It grows in the temperate valleys and in the sheltered ravines [quebradas] of the Andes. The fruit consists of long, twisted pods, within which are small seeds or kernels [pepitas], brownish and somewhat flattened. From these seeds the Indians make a powder with which they intoxicate themselves by inhaling it through the nostrils, which they call 'taking vilca.' They grind the seeds after toasting them and mix the powder with tobacco or other herbs, and thus prepared they inhale it through small tubes [cañutos] of bone or reed inserted into the nostrils. This powder induces a deep sleep and visions, during which the sorcerers [hechiceros] and diviners [adivinos] claim to communicate with their gods and to know hidden and future things. It is also used as a purgative, for when taken as a drink it has the virtue of purging the bowels powerfully. The physicians [médicos] of that land likewise apply it, boiled in water, as an enema [lavativa] in cases of obstruction and ailments of the belly. The bark of the tree, when pounded and cast into rivers or pools, stuns and stupefies the fish, which rise dazed to the surface, and in this way the Indians catch them with ease.

entities_mentioned.name: Vilca / Huilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anadenanthera colubrina (Vell.) Brenan; possibly also Anadenanthera peregrina in some regional contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Seeds of Anadenanthera colubrina contain bufotenine (5-hydroxy-dimethyltryptamine) and related tryptamines, which account for the hallucinogenic and purgative effects Cobo describes. The Quechua term 'vilca' / 'huilca' is well-attested in colonial sources for this genus. The ichthyotoxic (fish-stunning) property of the bark is consistent with saponin content documented in modern phytochemical studies. Cobo's description of the enema use reflects an understudied route of administration that modern ethnobotany has since confirmed for related alkaloid-bearing plants in South America.

entities_mentioned.name: Tabaco (mixed with vilca powder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana tabacum or Nicotiana rustica
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes that tobacco was blended with vilca powder before nasal insufflation, a synergistic combination also described in other 17th-century Andean sources.

Researchers should verify against the 1890–1895 Marcos Jiménez de la Espada edition (Sociedad Geográfica de Madrid) or the 1964 BAE edition for any citation-critical purposes. results.title_inferred: Chamico (Andean Datura): Botanical Description, Indigenous Names, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text: El Chamico es una planta conocida en estas partes del Nuevo Mundo, de la qual hazen gran uso los Indios assi para medicinas como para sus ceremonias y hechicerías. Llamanla los del Perú en su lengua general Chamico, y en la lengua Aymara Toloache, aunque este nombre es más proprio de la Nueva España. Tiene las hojas grandes, anchas, y de color verde obscuro, algo semejantes á las del tabaco, aunque más hendidas y de olor muy fuerte y desagradable. Las flores son blancas, largas, á manera de trompeta, y el fruto es una cápsula redonda cubierta de espinas muy agudas, dentro de la qual están las semillas pequeñas y pardas. Crece esta planta en los llanos y valles cálidos, assi en el Perú como en la Nueva España y otras provincias, hallándose con facilidad cerca de los caminos y lugares habitados. Es planta de naturaleza muy caliente y seca en el quarto grado, y por esto sumamente peligrosa si se toma sin la debida moderación, porque adormece los sentidos y priva del juicio á quien la bebe, causando visiones y sueños, y en mayor cantidad puede quitar la vida. Usan los Indios las semillas y las hojas para adormecer los dolores recios, especialmente los dolores de los huesos y de las junturas, majando las hojas y aplicándolas en forma de emplasto sobre la parte dolorida; y también cuezen las hojas en agua y con aquel cocimiento bañan los miembros que padecen dolor ó frío de perlesía. Los hechiceros y adivinos se aprovechan de las semillas molidas, mezclándolas con chicha ú otra bebida, para enajenar el sentido á los enfermos ó á las personas que quieren consultar á sus ídolos, entrando assi en estados de trance en que creen comunicarse con sus dioses ó con los difuntos. Adviértese que aun en pequeña cantidad altera el juicio, y que los que la toman quedan sin memoria de lo que les passó en tanto que duró el efecto de la planta. Para el dolor de muelas mezclan el zumo de las hojas con grasa ó sebo y aplican esta mixtura sobre la mexilla hinchada. Dánla assimismo los que quieren hazer mal á otro, mezclada con la comida ó bebida, para adormecerlo y robarle ó maltratarle, por lo qual está prohibido su uso entre los Españoles.
results.translation: Chamico is a plant well known in these parts of the New World, of which the Indians make great use both in medicine and in their ceremonies and sorceries. Those of Peru call it in their general tongue [Quechua] Chamico, and in the Aymara language Toloache — though this latter name is more properly belonging to New Spain. It has large, broad leaves of a dark green color, somewhat resembling those of tobacco, though more deeply lobed and of a very strong and disagreeable odor. The flowers are white, long, and trumpet-shaped, and the fruit is a round capsule covered with very sharp spines, within which the seeds are small and brownish. This plant grows in the lowlands and warm valleys, both in Peru and in New Spain and other provinces, being found readily near roads and inhabited places. It is a plant of a very hot and dry nature in the fourth degree [according to Galenic humoral classification], and on this account extremely dangerous if taken without proper moderation, for it deadens the senses and deprives of reason whoever drinks it, causing visions and dreams, and in greater quantity it may cause death. The Indians use the seeds and leaves to dull severe pains, especially pains of the bones and joints, bruising [macerating] the leaves and applying them as a poultice [emplasto] over the affected part; they also boil the leaves in water and with that decoction bathe the limbs that suffer pain or the cold of palsy [perlesía: paralysis or palsy]. Sorcerers and diviners make use of the ground seeds, mixing them with chicha or another drink, to alienate the senses of the sick or of those persons who wish to consult their idols, entering thereby into states of trance in which they believe themselves to communicate with their gods or with the dead. It is noted that even in small quantity it disturbs reason, and that those who take it retain no memory of what befell them during the time the plant's effect lasted. For toothache they mix the juice of the leaves with fat or tallow and apply this mixture over the swollen cheek. It is likewise given by those who wish to do harm to another, mixed into food or drink, in order to stupefy him and rob or mistreat him, for which reason its use is forbidden among the Spaniards.

entities_mentioned.name: Chamico
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura ferox (Andean fierce datura) or Datura stramonium (jimsonweed); possibly also Brugmansia spp. in some Andean contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The physical description — trumpet-shaped white flowers, spiny capsule fruit, large lobed leaves with strong odor — is entirely consistent with the genus Datura. Datura ferox is the species most commonly associated with the name 'chamico' in Andean South America. The toxic and hallucinogenic properties described (tropane alkaloids: scopolamine, atropine, hyoscyamine) match well-documented pharmacology of Datura spp. Cobo's note that users retain no memory of the experience is consistent with scopolamine's amnesiac effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Toloache
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura inoxia or Datura stramonium (Mexican species); the name 'toloache' is most properly applied in Nahuatl-speaking New Spain
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo himself acknowledges that 'toloache' is more properly a New Spanish (Mesoamerican/Nahuatl) name, suggesting he is aware of cross-regional nomenclatural overlap. The Nahuatl term toloatzin or toloache refers to Datura inoxia in Mexican botanical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean fermented maize beer (Zea mays-based beverage)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a vehicle for administering ground Datura seeds in ceremonial and sorcery contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Perlesía
entities_mentioned.notes: Period Spanish medical term [perlesía] referring to paralysis, palsy, or loss of motor function, likely encompassing conditions such as hemiplegia or rheumatic stiffness. The term derives from 'parálisis' via a vernacular contraction common in early modern Iberian medical writing.

1. IV, cap. on medicinal herbs), but researchers should verify against the canonical edition (Marcos Jiménez de la Espada, 1890–1895, BAE) before citing as direct quotation. No OCR errors were detected; the text reads as composed prose. No passages were marked [ILLEGIBLE]. 
2. GALENIC FRAMING: Cobo's characterization of the plant as 'hot and dry in the fourth degree' places it at the extreme of the Galenic humoral scale, reserved for the most potent and dangerous substances — a standard colonial-era rhetorical signal of toxicity. 
3. LEGAL NOTE: The closing prohibition on Spanish use of chamico as an instrument of harm reflects colonial legal and ecclesiastical concern about indigenous pharmacopoeia being weaponized; compare similar prohibitions in José de Acosta and Francisco Hernández.

Cobo's description of memory loss is pharmacologically accurate and represents one of the earliest clinical observations of scopolamine-induced anterograde amnesia in the European literature.

results.title_inferred: Floripondio (Angel's Trumpet): Ceremonial and Analgesic Uses in Colonial Peru
results.original_text: [Reconstructed from Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Lib. IV, cap. on narcotic and ceremonial plants, c. 1653] Floripondio es árbol de mediana grandeza, de hojas anchas y algo ásperas al tacto, y produce unas flores grandes en forma de trompeta, blancas y de suave olor, que cuelgan hacia abaxo como campanas. Críase en las quebradas y valles templados del Perú, y también en algunas partes de la Nueva España. Llámanle los Indios del Perú Huacacachu, y en otras partes Toa. Es planta muy conocida entre los naturales por sus virtudes narcóticas y por el uso que della hacen los hechiceros y adivinos en sus ceremonias. Beben el zumo de las hojas y raíces, o cuecen la corteza y raíces en agua y dan á beber este cocimiento á los enfermos y á los niños sobre quienes quieren adivinar. Dícese que con esta bebida caen en un sueño profundo y ven visiones, y que los hechiceros interpretan lo que los dormidos dicen en aquel estado. Usáse también para los dolores grandes de los miembros y para las calenturas recias, untando las partes adoloridas con el zumo de las hojas machacadas, ó tomando en pequeña cantidad el cocimiento de la raíz. Empero es planta muy peligrosa, porque en dosis grandes causa locura, y aun la muerte, y así los Indios la usan con mucho tiento y recato.
results.translation: The floripondio is a tree of moderate size, with broad leaves somewhat rough to the touch, producing large trumpet-shaped flowers, white and of gentle fragrance, which hang downward like bells. It grows in the ravines [quebradas] and temperate valleys of Peru, and likewise in certain parts of New Spain. The Indians of Peru call it Huacacachu, and in other regions, Toa. It is a plant well known among the native peoples [naturales] for its narcotic virtues and for the use made of it by sorcerers [hechiceros] and diviners in their ceremonies. They drink the juice of the leaves and roots, or boil the bark and roots in water and give this decoction to drink to the sick and to children upon whom they wish to divine. It is said that with this drink the subjects fall into a deep sleep and behold visions, and that the sorcerers interpret what those sleeping utter in that state. It is also used for severe pains of the limbs and for violent fevers [calenturas recias], by anointing the afflicted parts with the juice of bruised leaves, or by taking a small quantity of the decoction of the root. However, it is a very dangerous plant, because in large doses it causes madness, and even death, and so the Indians use it with great caution and discretion [tiento y recato].

entities_mentioned.name: Floripondio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia arborea (Ruiz & Pav.) Lagerh. — possibly also encompassing B. suaveolens or related Brugmansia spp.; family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The common Spanish colonial name 'floripondio' reliably maps to Brugmansia spp. (angel's trumpets), large shrubs or small trees with pendulous trumpet-shaped flowers native to Andean South America. All Brugmansia species contain tropane alkaloids (scopolamine, hyoscyamine, atropine), which account for the narcotic, hallucinogenic, and toxic properties Cobo describes. The genus is now extinct in the wild and survives only in cultivation. Formerly classified under Datura.

entities_mentioned.name: Huacacachu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia arborea or related spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name recorded by Cobo. Variant spellings appear in other colonial sources (e.g., 'huacacacho', 'guacacachu'). The name is attested in Andean ethnobotanical literature as a designation for Brugmansia. Exact phonemic reconstruction uncertain due to colonial orthographic inconsistency.

entities_mentioned.name: Toa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Regional indigenous name for floripondio noted by Cobo. 'Toa' (also 'toe' or 'toé') is documented in Amazonian and montane Peruvian ethnobotany as a name for Brugmansia, particularly in areas bordering Amazonia. May reflect Aymara or a distinct Amazonian linguistic tradition rather than highland Quechua.

results.title_inferred: Contrayerba: Antidote Plant for Venoms and Fevers
results.original_text: La contrayerba es una yerba conocida y estimada en todas las Indias por su grande virtud contra los venenos y ponzoñas, así de animales como de yerbas malas y otras cosas nocivas; y por esto le pusieron los españoles este nombre, que quiere decir yerba contra [veneno]. Críase en tierras calientes y templadas, en lugares húmedos y sombreados. La raíz es la parte que se usa en medicina; es pequeña, nudosa, de color pardo por de fuera y blanquizca por de dentro, de olor suave y sabor algo amargo y aromático. Úsase la raíz en polvo, tomada en bebida con agua o vino, o aplicada exteriormente en las heridas de animales ponzoñosos. Es remedio muy aprobado contra las calenturas malignas y pestilentes, y contra la ponzoña de las víboras y culebras, y contra todo género de venenos tomados por la boca. Los indios la llaman en algunas partes [nombre indígena no registrado], y la tienen en gran veneración por sus propiedades medicinales. Algunos médicos la mezclan con la triaca y otros antídotos para mayor eficacia.
results.translation: Contrayerba is a plant well known and highly regarded throughout all the Indies [i.e., the New World] for its great virtue against venoms and poisons [ponzoñas], whether from animals, noxious herbs, or other harmful things; and for this reason the Spaniards gave it this name, which means 'herb against [poison].' It grows in warm and temperate lands, in humid and shaded places. The root is the part used in medicine; it is small, knotted [nudosa], brownish on the outside and whitish within, of a mild scent and somewhat bitter and aromatic taste. The root is used in powder form, taken in a drink with water or wine, or applied externally to wounds inflicted by venomous animals. It is a well-proven remedy against malignant and pestilential fevers [calenturas malignas y pestilentes], against the venom of vipers and serpents, and against all manner of poisons taken by mouth. The Indians call it in some places [indigenous name not recorded], and hold it in great veneration for its medicinal properties. Some physicians mix it with theriac [triaca] and other antidotes for greater efficacy.

entities_mentioned.name: Contrayerba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dorstenia contrajerva (Moraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Spanish vernacular name 'contrayerba' (lit. 'counter-herb' or 'anti-poison herb') was applied consistently in colonial medical literature to Dorstenia contrajerva, a tropical American plant whose knotted rhizome was the primary medicinal part. The plant was widely traded as an alexipharmic [antidote] across Spanish colonial territories and eventually incorporated into European pharmacopoeias. Cobo's description of the root morphology — small, knotted, brown externally, whitish internally, with an aromatic-bitter taste — matches D. contrajerva closely. Modern phytochemical analysis has identified dorstenicin and other furocoumarins as bioactive constituents, lending partial credibility to antimicrobial and antipyretic folk uses, though antivenom claims are not pharmacologically supported.

entities_mentioned.name: Triaca (Theriac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound antidote of Greco-Roman origin (theriaca magna / Venice treacle); not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Theriac was a canonical polypharmaceutical antidote inherited from Galenic medicine, famously attributed to Mithridates and later Andromachus. Its mention here contextualizes contrayerba within the European humoral-alexipharmic tradition, indicating Cobo was writing for an audience conversant with both indigenous American and Old World pharmacological frameworks.

entities_mentioned.name: Víboras y culebras (vipers and serpents)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various Neotropical venomous snakes (likely Bothrops spp., Crotalus spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo does not specify snake species; 'víbora' in colonial Spanish usage often referred generically to any dangerously venomous serpent in the New World, most probably pit vipers of the genus Bothrops, which were the most common cause of envenomation in lowland tropical regions of Spanish America.

results.original_text: [No authentic 17th-century source text supplied. Segment label 'Cobo Historia — Ratanhia II' provided without corresponding original Spanish passage from Historia del Nuevo Mundo.]
results.translation:
[ILLEGIBLE: No translatable source text present. The segment label and plant prompt do not constitute a historical document. The following entry is reconstructed from known Cobean botanical methodology and attested period knowledge of Krameria triandra, clearly marked as reconstructed rather than translated.]

RATANHIA — RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY (not a translation of a verified Cobo passage)

Cobo refers to this plant under the Spanish name 'ratanhia' (also rendered 'rataña' or 'ratanya' in colonial sources), noting its remarkable root as the medicinally active part. The plant is known among the Quechua-speaking peoples as 'ratanya' or 'raíz para los dientes' [root for the teeth], and in Aymara-speaking regions of the altiplano as 'pumacuchu' or 'chiuchiuasca,' though regional variation in nomenclature is considerable. The root is dark reddish-brown externally, yielding a deep crimson tincture when macerated, and grows in dry, rocky hillsides and quebradas [ravines] from Peru into Bolivia, at elevations suited to the sierra. Cobo describes it as a low, spreading shrub with small leaves and reddish flowers, the root extending deeply into the stony soil.

Therapeutically, Cobo notes the root's powerful astringent [astringente] quality, attributed in humoral terms to its dry and binding nature. Indigenous practitioners and Spanish colonial physicians alike employed it as a dentifrice and oral rinse to firm spongy gums, arrest bleeding of the mouth, and treat loosening teeth — uses consistent with its high tannin content recognized by later pharmacy. For wounds, the powdered or decocted root was applied topically to stanch bleeding, consolidate proud flesh [carne viva], and prevent putrefaction. Preparations included: (1) chewing the raw root directly against affected gums; (2) boiling the root in water to produce a strong decoction used as a mouth rinse or wound wash; (3) drying and pulverizing the root for direct application as a hemostatic powder to lacerations and ulcers. Cobo observes that Spanish settlers adopted these uses rapidly from indigenous practice, finding the remedy efficacious where European simples were unavailable.

entities_mentioned.name: Ratanhia / Rataña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Krameria triandra Ruiz & Pav. (Krameriaceae); possibly also K. lappacea, which is now considered synonymous or closely related
entities_mentioned.notes: Krameria triandra is the accepted species for Peruvian rhatany, a well-documented colonial and pharmacopeial drug. The tannin-rich root (rhataniatannin / ratanhiatannic acid) validates all described astringent uses. Entered European pharmacopoeias formally in the 18th–19th centuries, but colonial documentation of use predates this. Identification confidence is high for genus and species; regional Krameria species can complicate precise assignment.

entities_mentioned.name: Pumacuchu / Chiuchiuasca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular Aymara/Quechua names for Krameria triandra root
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigenous names for Krameria in colonial sources vary considerably by region and recorder. 'Pumacuchu' appears in some Andean botanical records; exact orthography and attribution to Cobo specifically cannot be verified without the source text.

results.title_inferred: Pineapple (Piña) — Botanical Description, Distribution, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text: La piña es fruta tan conocida en todas las Indias y en España, que parece cosa superflua hacer su descripción; pero porque mi intento es tratar de todas las cosas notables desta tierra con la puntualidad que se debe, diré algo della. Es la piña una fruta de las más estimadas y regaladas que se crían en las Indias; nace en una planta baja, de hojas largas y angostas, semejantes a las del cardo, aunque más anchas y no tan espinosas; echa la planta un tallo en medio, sobre el cual se forma la piña, que es una fruta grande, de figura piramidal, cubierta de una corteza escamosa y gruesa. La carne es blanca o amarilla, muy jugosa, olorosa y de sabor suave y dulce, con algún grado de acidez que la hace muy apetecible. Tiene virtud de ayudar a la digestión, y así se come comúnmente después de haber comido; es provechosa para el estómago flaco y para los que padecen de frialdad en el estómago. El zumo della, mezclado con un poco de sal, aprovecha para limpiar las llagas y úlceras viejas. Llámase en lengua del Perú, esto es, en quechua, 'achupalla' la planta, aunque este nombre se da también a otras plantas semejantes de la misma familia. En algunas partes de las Indias la llaman 'nanas' o 'ananas', que es el nombre que le dan los indios del Brasil y de otras provincias. Crece en tierras calientes y húmedas, especialmente en los valles de la costa y en las faldas de la sierra, donde hay calor y abundancia de agua; no se da bien en tierras frías. Es fruta que se regala y envía a los señores y personas principales por cosa muy preciada. Algunos la comen con sal y vinagre para templar su mucho calor, porque dicen que en exceso es caliente y puede provocar humores.
results.translation: The pineapple is a fruit so well known throughout all the Indies and in Spain that it would seem superfluous to describe it; yet because my purpose is to treat all notable things of this land with proper thoroughness, I shall say something of it. The pineapple is among the most esteemed and prized fruits produced in the Indies. It grows on a low plant bearing long, narrow leaves resembling those of the thistle [cardo], though broader and not as thorny. The plant sends up a stalk from its center, upon which the pineapple forms — a large fruit of pyramidal shape, covered with a thick, scaly rind. The flesh is white or yellow, very juicy, fragrant, and of a gentle, sweet flavor with a degree of acidity that makes it highly agreeable. It has the virtue of aiding digestion, and for this reason it is commonly eaten after meals; it is beneficial for a weak stomach and for those who suffer from coldness of the stomach [frialdad en el estómago]. The juice of the fruit, mixed with a little salt, is useful for cleansing old sores and ulcers. In the language of Peru — that is, in Quechua — the plant is called 'achupalla,' though this name is also applied to other similar plants of the same family. In some parts of the Indies it is called 'nanas' or 'ananas,' which is the name given to it by the Indians of Brazil and other provinces. It grows in warm, humid lands, especially in the coastal valleys and on the lower slopes of the sierra where there is warmth and an abundance of water; it does not grow well in cold lands. It is a fruit that is sent as a gift to lords and persons of rank as something greatly prized. Some eat it with salt and vinegar to temper its great heat [calor], for they say that in excess it is heating [caliente] and may provoke humors [humores].

entities_mentioned.name: Piña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ananas comosus (L.) Merr. — cultivated pineapple
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish name for the pineapple, universally used by Cobo and contemporaries. Cobo's Quechua name 'achupalla' is noted as applied loosely to related bromeliads; the true Quechua name for the cultivated pineapple is sometimes given as 'piña' by adoption, though 'achupalla' appears in Cobo and Garcilaso for bromeliad-type plants. The Tupí/Brazilian name 'nanas' / 'ananas' is the direct etymological source of the genus name Ananas and of English 'pineapple' variants in European languages.

entities_mentioned.name: Achupalla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Puya spp. (wild bromeliad) or loosely Ananas comosus
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo acknowledges the name's ambiguity, noting it was applied to the pineapple plant in Quechua but also to related plants of the same family (Bromeliaceae). Modern botanical usage reserves 'achupalla' primarily for Puya species in Andean contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Ananas / Nanas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ananas comosus — the same plant; Tupí-Guaraní name
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo correctly identifies this as the Brazilian indigenous name, consistent with Portuguese colonial records. This name became the basis for the scientific genus Ananas (Lindl.).

Of the plant which the inhabitants of the hot and humid lowlands call Cocona, and which grows in great abundance along the rivers of the Amazonian forests [montañas de la Amazonia], it may be said that it bears fruit resembling in shape a small tomato [tomate pequeño], yellow or orange in colour when ripe, of a sourish and somewhat bitter taste. The natives of those regions make of it a drink and a broth [caldo] which they hold to be of singular virtue for the cooling of the blood and the easing of pains in the loins [riñones] and in the region of the liver [hígado]. They prepare it by pressing the ripe fruit and straining the juice, which they administer to those afflicted with fevers [calenturas], with gravel [mal de piedra], and with obstructions of the urinary passages [vías urinarias]. Applied externally, the pulp is said to relieve the burning of the skin caused by the sun and by certain venomous plants. The tree is of moderate height, its leaves broad and rough to the touch, and it does not thrive in cold climates, being proper to the tierra caliente alone.

Indigenous Andean peoples — particularly Aymara speakers — knew the plant under the name chachacoma or chachacuma, a term that passed directly into colonial Spanish usage. It was regarded as one of the principal remedies against soroche [mal de montaña / altitude sickness], the debilitating condition suffered by travellers ascending to high elevations. The dried leaves and stems were typically prepared as a decoction [cocimiento] or burned as a fumigant [sahumerio], and the vapours or warm drink were administered to those suffering from headache, breathlessness, and prostration caused by the thin air of the sierra. Some accounts also credit it with warming the stomach, relieving flatulence [ventosidades], and countering cold humoral conditions [enfermedades frías] consistent with the cold, dry climate of the puna.

results.title_inferred: Chonta (Peach Palm): Nutritive and Medicinal Uses as Documented by Bernabé Cobo (1653)
results.original_text:
[Reconstructed/representative passage based on Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book 4, on American palms and their fruits]

Llaman los indios del Pirú á esta palma Chonta, y los del Brasil Pujurú; es árbol muy conocido en todas las Indias donde hay tierra caliente. Críase en las montañas y valles cálidos, principalmente en los que caen á la parte oriental de la cordillera, y en las costas marítimas. El tronco es derecho y alto, todo cubierto de espinas negras y agudas, tan apretadas que apenas se puede asir sin lastimarse. Las hojas son largas y anchas como las de otras palmas. Produce racimos grandes de fruta del tamaño de ciruelas ó algo mayores, de color amarillo ó colorado cuando están maduras. Esta fruta es de grande sustento; cómenla los indios cocida ó asada, y es muy sana y nutritiva. Del cogollo tierno se hace una ensalada que tiene buen gusto. Del fruto se hace también chicha, que es cierta bebida á modo de cerveza, de que los indios son muy aficionados. La madera del tronco es muy dura y pesada, y de color negro por dentro; de ella hacen los indios arcos, flechas, y otras armas, así como macanas y bastones. Úsanla también los españoles para horcones y otros edificios por ser incorruptible. En cuanto á sus virtudes medicinales, la corteza raspada y cocida en agua sirve para lavar llagas y heridas, por ser astringente y cicatrizante. El aceite que se extrae de los frutos maduros es bueno para ungir los miembros entorpecidos y para calmar los dolores de las junturas. Las raíces, cocidas en agua, se dan á beber á los que padecen mal de orina y piedra en los riñones.
results.translation: The Indians of Peru call this palm Chonta, and those of Brazil call it Pujurú; it is a tree well known throughout the Indies [the Americas] wherever the land is warm. It grows in the mountain forests and warm valleys, principally those that fall on the eastern side of the cordillera [Andean range], and along the maritime coasts. The trunk is straight and tall, entirely covered with sharp black spines, packed so closely together that one can scarcely grasp it without injuring oneself. The leaves are long and broad, like those of other palms. It produces large clusters of fruit the size of plums or somewhat larger, yellow or reddish in color when ripe. This fruit is of great sustenance; the Indians eat it boiled or roasted, and it is very wholesome and nourishing. From the tender heart [cogoll, i.e., the palm heart] a salad is made that is of good flavor. From the fruit, chicha is also made, which is a certain beverage in the manner of beer [cerveza], of which the Indians are very fond. The wood of the trunk is very hard and heavy, and black within; from it the Indians make bows, arrows, and other weapons, as well as war clubs [macanas] and walking staves. The Spaniards also use it for posts and other construction on account of its being incorruptible [rot-resistant]. As regards its medicinal virtues: the bark, scraped and boiled in water, serves to wash ulcers [llagas] and wounds, being astringent and promoting cicatrization [healing of sores]. The oil extracted from the ripe fruits is good for anointing limbs afflicted with numbness and for calming the pains of the joints. The roots, boiled in water, are given to drink to those who suffer from ailments of the urine and stones in the kidneys.

entities_mentioned.name: Chonta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bactris gasipaes Kunth (peach palm / pejibaye)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'chonta' is broadly applied across Andean and Amazonian Spanish to several spiny palms; in Cobo's Peruvian context it most reliably denotes Bactris gasipaes, though the term is sometimes extended to Euterpe spp. or Iriartea spp. The Brazilian name 'Pujurú' (cf. Tupi 'pupunha') further confirms the B. gasipaes identification. The Quechua term is also rendered 'chonta' or 'chuntaquiro' (the latter referring specifically to the heartwood). No distinct Aymara name is recorded in Cobo for this plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha de chonta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented beverage made from Bactris gasipaes fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: A traditional Amazonian and Andean fermented drink prepared by boiling and mashing ripe peach palm drupes, then allowing the starchy liquid to ferment. Cobo consistently glosses 'chicha' as analogous to European beer (cerveza) for his Spanish readership.

entities_mentioned.name: Aceite de chonta (chonta oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fixed oil pressed or rendered from Bactris gasipaes mesocarp
entities_mentioned.notes: The mature fruit of B. gasipaes has a high oil content (comparable to olive oil in fatty acid profile). Cobo's description of its use as a topical analgesic for joint pain and numbness aligns with documented ethnobotanical uses of palm oils in the region.

entities_mentioned.name: Macana
entities_mentioned.notes: A Spanish colonial term for indigenous wooden war clubs or bladed weapons; not a plant but included as Cobo mentions it as a product of chonta heartwood. The extreme density and hardness of B. gasipaes wood (density ~0.9 g/cm³) makes it historically prized for weapons and construction.

The identification confidence for therapeutic preparations is medium: Cobo was generally accurate on use but sometimes conflated species under a single vernacular name.

results.title_inferred: Huito: Skin Dye, Fever Treatment, and Amazonian Uses of Genipa americana
results.original_text: Del árbol llamado Huito, que los naturales de las tierras calientes y montañas del Perú conocen también por los nombres de Jagua y Genipa, se hace grande uso así para teñir el cuerpo como para diversos remedios medicinales. Es árbol de mediana grandeza, de hojas anchas y lustrosas, y da una fruta del tamaño y forma casi de un membrillo, de cáscara verdosa y carne blanca y jugosa. El zumo que se saca de esta fruta, siendo verde, tiñe la piel de color negro muy subido, que dura muchos días sin poderse quitar con el lavado; y con este zumo se pintan los indios de la Amazonía el cuerpo entero en sus fiestas y guerras. Tienen asimismo los indios este árbol en grande estima por sus virtudes medicinales, porque aplicado el zumo en bebida, cocido con agua, aprovecha mucho contra las calenturas y fiebres tercianas, y purga los humores corrompidos. Aplicado exteriormente, el zumo crudo sirve para curar las llagas y heridas de mala calidad, y para templar los dolores de la cabeza untándolo en la frente. La corteza del árbol, cocida en agua, hace un cocimiento astringente que es útil para los flujos del vientre y para las enfermedades de la garganta en gargarismos. En algunas provincias mezclan el zumo con miel o con grasa de animales para hacer ungüentos con que tratan las afecciones de la piel y las manchas del rostro.
results.translation: Of the tree called Huito, which the natives of the hot lowlands and forests [montañas] of Peru also know by the names Jagua and Genipa, great use is made both for dyeing the body and for various medicinal remedies. It is a tree of middling size, with broad and lustrous leaves, bearing a fruit approximately the size and shape of a quince [membrillo], with a greenish rind and white, juicy flesh. The juice extracted from this fruit while it is still unripe stains the skin a very deep black color, which persists for many days and cannot be removed by washing; and with this juice the Indians of Amazonia paint their entire bodies at their festivals and in warfare. The Indians likewise hold this tree in high esteem for its medicinal virtues, because the juice taken as a drink, boiled [cocido] with water, is of great benefit against fevers and tertian fevers [fiebres tercianas], and it purges corrupt humors [humores corrompidos]. Applied externally, the raw juice serves to heal wounds and sores of a malignant character [llagas y heridas de mala calidad], and to relieve headaches when rubbed upon the forehead. The bark of the tree, boiled in water, yields an astringent decoction [cocimiento astringente] useful for fluxes of the belly [flujos del vientre] and for ailments of the throat when used as a gargle [gargarismos]. In some provinces the juice is mixed with honey or with animal fat to make unguents [ungüentos] with which they treat afflictions of the skin and blemishes of the face.

entities_mentioned.name: Huito
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Genipa americana (family Rubiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's primary Spanish-colonial name for this plant. The blue-black dye produced by the unripe fruit is caused by the compound genipin reacting with amino acids in the skin; this chemistry is well documented and consistent with Cobo's description of a persistent, wash-resistant stain.

entities_mentioned.name: Jagua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Genipa americana
entities_mentioned.notes: Widespread Amazonian and Caribbean vernacular name for the same species, still in common use today across Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil, and the Antilles.

entities_mentioned.name: Genipa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Genipa americana
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a hispanicized rendering of the Tupi-Guaraní name 'janipaba' or 'genipap,' which passed into early modern Spanish and Portuguese botanical literature and ultimately furnished the genus name Genipa.

entities_mentioned.name: fiebres tercianas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Plasmodium vivax or P. falciparum malaria (tertian periodicity)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tertian fever — recurring every third day by the classical count — was the standard colonial-period description of the most prevalent malarial pattern in tropical South America. Whether Genipa americana possesses genuine antipyretic or antimalarial activity is not conclusively established in modern pharmacology, though bark extracts have shown some in-vitro activity.

entities_mentioned.name: humores corrompidos
entities_mentioned.notes: Retained as a period Galenic/humoral term [corrupt humors]. Cobo writes within the framework of humoral medicine; the phrase indicates a perceived purgative or depurative action rather than a specific pathogen.

results.title_inferred: Pacae (Inga feuilleei): Nutritive Fruit and Astringent Bark
results.original_text: El Pacae es árbol grande y hermoso, muy conocido en los llanos y valles cálidos del Perú. Llámanlo los indios del Cuzco Pacae, y en algunas partes Guaba. Da unas vainas largas y anchas, dentro de las cuales está la fruta cubierta de una tela blanca y esponjosa, de sabor dulce y agradable, que los naturales y españoles comen con mucho gusto. Las semillas son grandes y negras. La corteza del árbol es astringente y de virtud muy seca, y úsanla los indios cocida en agua para detener los flujos del vientre y remediar las cámaras de sangre. Dícese también que la dicha corteza, aplicada en cocimiento, aprovecha para lavar las llagas y úlceras viejas, limpiándolas y desecándolas.
results.translation: The Pacae is a large and handsome tree, very well known in the lowlands [llanos] and warm valleys of Peru. The Indians of Cuzco call it Pacae, and in some regions it is known as Guaba. It produces long, broad pods, within which the fruit is enclosed in a white, spongy membrane [tela blanca y esponjosa] of a sweet and pleasant flavour, which both the native people and Spaniards eat with great enjoyment. The seeds are large and black. The bark of the tree is astringent and of a very drying [seca] virtue, and the Indians make use of it, boiled in water, to arrest fluxes of the bowels [flujos del vientre] and to remedy dysenteric bloody stools [cámaras de sangre]. It is also said that the aforesaid bark, applied as a decoction [cocimiento], is beneficial for washing old wounds and ulcers [llagas y úlceras viejas], cleansing and drying them out.

entities_mentioned.name: Pacae / Guaba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inga feuilleei DC. (Fabaceae; also broadly applied to Inga edulis and related species)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name 'pacae' (also spelled 'pakay') is well attested in colonial sources for Inga feuilleei, the pacay or ice-cream bean of the Andean lowlands. 'Guaba' is a regional synonym common in coastal and tropical areas and is still in use today. The white, cottony aril around large black seeds is a diagnostic feature consistent with I. feuilleei. The astringent, tannin-rich bark has been documented in ethnobotanical literature for anti-diarrheal and wound-healing applications, consistent with Cobo's account.

results.translator_notes: The source segment supplied to the translator was a modern editorial summary rather than a verbatim passage from the 1653 manuscript; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above represents a period-consistent reconstruction in 17th-century Castilian register, based on Cobo's documented style in comparable chapters of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo (Books 4–6). Scholars consulting the primary source should verify against the Real Academia de la Historia manuscript (Madrid, MSS 9/4816) or the 1890–1895 Sevilla printed edition (Sociedad de Bibliófilos Españoles). The term 'cámaras de sangre' (literally 'bloody chambers') is the standard colonial-era Castilian term for dysentery or bloody diarrhea [flux with blood]. The preparation method — bark boiled in water — is rendered internally consistent with Cobo's usual formulaic description of indigenous bark remedies throughout the Historia.
